Name: info_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limit of model compression can be characterized by a rate-distortion function, which balances the trade-off between compression ratio and predictive sufficiency. By deriving achievable and converse bounds, we can identify the optimal compression strategy that minimizes the description length of the model while preserving its predictive performance.
Related Work: Recent works have explored model compression techniques such as quantization, pruning, and knowledge distillation. However, these methods often rely on heuristics or empirical evaluations, lacking a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal distinguishes from existing literature by establishing a theoretical framework for model compression, leveraging tools from rate-distortion theory and information theory to derive fundamental limits and optimal compression strategies.
Abstract: This project aims to establish a theoretical framework for model compression, leveraging information-theoretic tools to derive fundamental limits and optimal compression strategies. We will investigate the rate-distortion function of model compression, characterizing the trade-off between compression ratio and predictive sufficiency. By deriving achievable and converse bounds, we will identify the optimal compression strategy that minimizes the description length of the model while preserving its predictive performance. Our approach will provide a rigorous foundation for model compression, enabling the development of more efficient and effective compression techniques.
Experiments: Derive achievable and converse bounds for model compression using rate-distortion theory
Evaluate the performance of existing compression techniques (e.g., quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation) in relation to the derived bounds
Develop and test novel compression algorithms that optimize the trade-off between compression ratio and predictive sufficiency
Investigate the application of our framework to various machine learning models and tasks
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving tight bounds for model compression may limit the applicability of our framework to certain models or tasks
The development of novel compression algorithms may require significant computational resources and expertise
The evaluation of our framework may be influenced by the choice of models, tasks, and evaluation metrics
